event: Hunter-gatherer communities settle in the area of present-day Burkina Faso, leaving stone tools across the savanna region.
Neolithic farming and herding communities establish settlements, cultivating crops and domesticating animals on the Sahelian plateau.
Iron-working spreads through the region, with furnaces and smelting sites marking the rise of metallurgy in West Africa.
Settled agricultural villages with pottery and iron tools flourish across the Mossi Plateau and Volta basin.
The Dogon and other early peoples inhabit the region before being displaced or absorbed by later migrations.
Trans-Saharan trade routes link the region to the Mali Empire and North Africa, exchanging gold, salt, and kola nuts.
The Mossi peoples establish a series of powerful kingdoms on the central plateau, founding the Ouagadougou and Yatenga states.
Mossi cavalry raid the city of Timbuktu, demonstrating the military reach of the emerging Mossi kingdoms.
The Mossi Kingdom of Ouagadougou consolidates under the Mogho Naba, the paramount ruler whose dynasty would endure for centuries.
Mossi forces clash with the expanding Songhai Empire as both powers contest control of the Niger bend region.
Songhai emperor Askia Muhammad launches a jihad against the Mossi, who resist Islamization and retain their traditional religion.
The Gurma, Bobo, Lobi, and other peoples maintain distinct societies alongside the dominant Mossi states.
The Mossi kingdoms reach their height, organized into a hierarchical system of provinces under the Mogho Naba.
The region serves as a source and transit zone for captives in the trans-Saharan and Atlantic slave trades.
French explorer Louis-Gustave Binger travels through Mossi territory, gathering intelligence for French colonial expansion.
French forces conquer the Mossi kingdom of Ouagadougou, capturing the capital and forcing the Mogho Naba into submission.
France establishes control over most of the territory, defeating the remaining Mossi and neighboring states.
The territory is incorporated into French West Africa as part of the colony of Upper Senegal and Niger.
The Volta-Bani anti-colonial war erupts as local peoples revolt against French taxation, conscription, and forced labor.
France creates the separate colony of Upper Volta (Haute-Volta), with Ouagadougou as its administrative center.
France dissolves the colony of Upper Volta, partitioning its territory among Ivory Coast, French Sudan, and Niger.
Upper Volta is reconstituted as a separate French territory following lobbying by Mossi leaders and the Mogho Naba.
Upper Volta becomes a self-governing republic within the French Community after a referendum.
Upper Volta gains full independence from France on 5 August, with Maurice Yameogo as its first president.
Upper Volta is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
A popular uprising and military coup overthrow President Yameogo, bringing Colonel Sangoule Lamizana to power.
A new constitution establishes a mixed civilian-military government under Lamizana's continued leadership.
Colonel Saye Zerbo seizes power in a coup, ending Lamizana's long rule amid economic discontent and labor unrest.
Another military coup brings Major Jean-Baptiste Ouedraogo to power, deepening political instability.
Captain Thomas Sankara leads a revolutionary coup, becoming president and launching a radical reform program.
Sankara renames the country Burkina Faso, meaning 'Land of Upright People,' and adopts a new flag and anthem.
Thomas Sankara is assassinated in a coup led by his comrade Blaise Compaore, who seizes the presidency.
A new constitution is adopted establishing the Fourth Republic, and Compaore wins a multiparty presidential election.
Investigative journalist Norbert Zongo is murdered, sparking mass protests against the Compaore government.
Burkina Faso continues mediating regional conflicts under Compaore, who positions himself as a West African peace broker.
Army mutinies and popular protests over high living costs erupt across the country, shaking Compaore's rule.
A popular uprising forces Blaise Compaore to resign after 27 years in power when he attempts to extend his term limits.
A short-lived coup by the presidential guard is reversed by popular resistance, and democratic elections bring Roch Marc Christian Kabore to power.
Jihadist militants attack a hotel and cafe in Ouagadougou, killing dozens and signaling an escalating Islamist insurgency.
Escalating jihadist violence displaces hundreds of thousands of people, creating a severe humanitarian crisis in the north and east.
Two military coups in one year topple President Kabore and then interim leader Damiba, bringing Captain Ibrahim Traore to power.
Burkina Faso forms the Alliance of Sahel States with Mali and Niger, pivoting away from France toward new partners.
Burkina Faso, Mali, and Niger announce their withdrawal from the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).
